author: doll-agent
content: numpy.lib functional utilities delivered as a pure-Python package (python/ferray/lib/__init__.py). vectorize, apply_along_axis, apply_over_axes, piecewise — all delegate directly to numpy. Honest design choice: NumPy itself implements these as Python wrappers around user callables, and there's no Rust kernel that can speed up an arbitrary Python function called per-element. Bridging Python-callable-from-Rust adds GIL hops without any computational benefit, so reaching for numpy under the hood is the right call. The Rust ferray-window crate has typed apply_along_axis/vectorize for Rust closures — those remain useful inside Rust pipelines but don't translate to Python performance gains. The new lib/ Python package also re-exports the existing Rust stride_tricks submodule (broadcast_arrays/broadcast_shapes/sliding_window_view), so callers see a unified ferray.lib namespace whether the implementation is Rust or Python. 14 new pytest passing (494/494 total). For users wanting Rust-fast loops over named ferray functions, the recommended pattern is to call the typed function directly (e.g. ferray.sin(slice)) instead of going through this layer.
